Reinforcement Learning and Decision Making

3.84 / 5 rating4.21 / 5 difficulty22.34 hrs / week

Quick Facts and Resources

Something missing or incorrect? Tell us more.

Name
Reinforcement Learning and Decision Making
Listed As
CS-7642
Credit Hours
3
Available to
CS students
Description
Efficient algorithms for multiagent planning, and approaches to learning near-optimal decisions using possibly partially observable Markov decision processes; stochastic and repeated games; and reinforcement learning.
Syllabus
Syllabus
Textbooks
  • Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction, 2nd Edition

  • /xBSUnnDc/9xllzRES6cvA==May 16, 2026spring 2026

    The RL course was very time consuming. As someone who works full time and was in the middle of a big move, I was struggling to keep up with the amount of time it took to fine-tune projects to be able to get good results. This was my fifth course in the ML track, and I took it at the same time as Software Development Process. I previously took AI for Robotics, Computer Vision, Machine Learning, and AI. All straight As, including RL and SDP.

    I think that we were the first semester (Spring 2026) that had the course assignments frontloaded. Given my other work commitments and move, I was not able to take advantage of that but wish I could, especially for the last project.

    Quizzes are open-book and have unlimited attempts. You basically have free points on these. Some quizzes needed many attempts to get one or two questions right as they are MCMA. Overall, easy points but they don't weigh much so not a huge piece here either. Some people missed out on their deadlines.

    P1 - P3 were okay projects. All projects take significant amount of hours to run (at least 20+). What was frustrating is the amount of time it takes to train your agents only to discover that your 5-hr or 10-hr run was for naught, and had worse results than your previous run. It felt like a never-ending cycle of fine-tuning in the dark with many of the projects, as beyond needing to write all code from scratch with no RL libraries allowed, there were some heavy restrictions on what you could or could not do when writing your code that would therefore would have made your agent faster and easier to solve, despite all being from scratch. So some of those weren't allowed, like speed adjustments to the agent, and made it much harder.

    I got an almost perfect score on P1, and got above 90 on the rest. On top of what is laid out requirements-wise for the report, there is a hidden rubric by the TAs, I believe. I found that it kind of depends on who you were graded by to dock points on things they wanted you to write more or less about.

    On P1 I was docked a few points on writing too little on hyperparams, although it was a significant section. Another project they docked off points for mentioning that too much and not enough on other things. I believe that all my reports had relatively balanced or logical portions.

    Overall I tried to write as much as possible on the most important topics, but found that writing the reports for RL were harder than ML or AI project reports, for example, due to their hidden expectations. Also, every report has to come with a video presentation (you don't have to show yourself), which I find is just unnecessary added workload for every project. They don't mention anything about your videos in your grading and I don't know what difference it actually makes.

    P4, AWS Deepracer, needs to become an optional project or be replaced. I was scared I might lose an A due to this and the possibility of doing badly on the final exam. The median on the final was 50%, but I got above 80%. I work in the field for many years, while also still cramming studying time.

    For P4, they initially only gave us 3 weeks to complete this project (unlike 4 weeks each for P1 - P3) but it wasn't nearly enough time. A lot of students were using the PACE servers but it was constantly down and people were waiting for 8+ hours on a PACE waitlist just to be able to run their project on GPU. There were also never-ending technical issues on PACE that students constantly reported on Ed even when they were able to use a PACE session. Due to this the TAs gave us another week penalty-free.

    Without that week I'd have not been able to solve at all. I was only able to solve, and only partially, near the end of the deadline, after running my CUDA-enabled NVIDIA GPU computer non-stop since the official beginning of P4, for almost 4 weeks. Discord was the only thing that saved me and even then I wasn't able to solve completely on all required portions of P4, and only solved three quarters of Part I's tracks. I was expecting them to dock points on that, and they did, but not too much. I still got almost 90% for that project. I had a well-written report and explained challenges in-depth.

    If you don't have a dedicated personal computer with a GPU, I'd strongly discourage you from doing this course. Or get one, FAST. You will simply not survive well on Google Colab or PACE, when there is a day's long waitlist for PACE + technical issues, and your runs take at least 5 hrs at a time.

    The curve for this course is very generous. The cut-off for getting an A was anyone who had an overall grade of 75% or above.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 40 hours / week

  • VZuizvJ61QQ8b9TKcMFtTQ==May 10, 2026spring 2026

    I really liked this class, but it left a lot more to be desired. RL is super cool and I really liked the content. I stopped watching the lectures after P2 to focus on the projects themselves.

    The projects were really fun and felt like it gave a really good hands on experience for RL. That being said, there were so many issues with P4 that it went from being fun and a reinforcement learning experience to a learning experience of how to set up the environment on different machines. I had never used PACE clusters in prior classes, but it was quite brutal. So much so that they granted a +1 week extension on P4, but unfortunately most of that extension was burned still waiting to get a reservation. I would encourage everyone to start P4 ASAP, but I also think the teaching staff should create an assignment where success is dependent on something so fragile

    The final exam was wild. I honestly don't think I would have gotten much of a better grade if I would have had an additional week to study

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 16 hours / week

  • ZduXZVe+NcBIRDua2dy92A==May 9, 2026spring 2026

    I'm writing this review before knowing my grade (by purpose – not to be biased by it). Depending on the score for the last project and the curve, it can be anywhere from C to A (I personally think/hope B is more likely). This is my 8th course, after AI, DL, ML, etc. - all A's. This class was a disappointment. Arguably the most important/useful subject in program was taught very poorly, or, rather, not taught at all. Lectures were inadequate. The teaching staff was simply not there to answer myriads of questions on the discussion board. Many students were screwed by being unable to have the proper setup for the last project and the environment was broken anyways, so even those who could set it up could not run meaningful experiments. While the projects overall were interesting, the report writing was taking a lot of time, shifting the focus from the important aspects of the experiments to making sure that all the check boxes are marked – and even then, something would be inadvertently missed.
    The grading of the reports was super formal, without any substantial feedback. Not sure what exactly the final exam was testing, there were no preparation materials or anything – just learn all the assigned reading by heart. Median grade for the final was 49, mean 49.9.
    I still think I learned a fair amount about RL while taking this class but not thanks to but despite how it was conducted. I could have learned much more and done that more efficiently. In a word, I think this class has been long calling for serious refurbishment. I was taking RL right after its sister class – ML. While that one was talking about 2x the time and the lectures were as outdated as here, the teaching staff there was super enthusiastic, supportive and helpful. It felt they were genuinely interested in students to pick up the topic. Nothing like that in RL – everything was very formal, detached and indifferent. At least I felt this way.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • nnI6r+dF+DJNIKDbjO2cmg==May 7, 2026spring 2026

    Withdrew from this course midway. I actually completed 3/4 projects before I decided to withdraw, so it was extremely disappointing.

    The course is composed of 1 final exam and 4 different projects - Traffic light simulator, Lunar Lander, Overcooked and AWS deepracers. There are 4 quizzes but those are unlimited attempts so they are essentially free points.

    For the very first project, I was able to complete it with good experimental results and put some effort into ensuring that the write-up was good. Even after all that, I landed up with 71.5/100. The feedback just mentioned something like "Good range of experiments... Could have written about xyz." I would have surely written about it if the instructions asked for it. The grading rubric was not shared, and you just had to guess what was important. The score is also pretty random, as they don't mention how they derive the number. I lost hope after seeing this grade. I submitted project 2 and was thinking until the last moment if I should withdraw. After the distribution came out, I realized there is no way I could get an A, so I withdrew. A few weeks after withdrawing, I saw my project 2 grade in Gradescope, and it was a 100.

    Writing a paper and recording a video are extremely frustrating in this class, and there is actually no need for both. Pretty sure the teaching staff didn't even care to grade the video. For the second project my video was 10mins instead of 5mins so I edited it to be 2x the speed. The content could still be understood but I was told by the TA that I should avoid doing it. They anyway gave a full grade.

    I would rather do the above mentioned projects on my own than take this class again. The youtube course lectures are anyway better and the grading is not going to help in any way. I didn't get to the exam but heard that it was another disaster.

    Some key things I would do for project 1:

    • Cite academic sources
    • They don't like Heatmaps although it gives a deeper understanding of the policy. So always stick to learning curves even.
    • Write even simple things like state tuple in mathematical notation.
    • Explain how the simulator is working

    I also decided to drop out of this program after taking this course and ML4T.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • FNSkyjhjdkDAZ1hbqpRZkQ==March 11, 2026fall 2025

    Check out the grade distribution, this class has gotten worse, people are getting more Cs and Ds now. Not recommended for people with a full time job. I was counting on "the curve" to get a C and graduate as it was my last class. There wasn't any curve that people speaks of during Fall 25. Office hours was a waste of time, lectures was useless. I should of use PPO for everything and it would of make my life much easier, but I chose to experiment with different algorithms. You will learn absolutely nothing from the teaching staff. Count on reading a lot: Sutton RL book end to end, MARL book, and the Grokking book for the algorithms. What you paid for is for someone to read your papers and give you a bad grade because you didn't meet their hidden rubrick. I dropped this course twice before during the registration period. I finally decide to go with it thinking the new staff is better. BIG MISTAKE. I got to take a 11th class now.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 30 hours / week

  • y95bmX+tFh3XCTGPOgZ78A==December 22, 2025fall 2025

    RL is my 6th course at GT (AI/AI4R/ML/CV/RL). I got an A. I felt as though the course had the following issues:

    • The readings are pulled from a free online book and quite long, covering several topics that aren't pertinent to the projects. Likewise the required lectures are not useful for the projects beyond week 2-3; most students recommend watching a different set of lectures (David Silver). Personally, I didn't find the supplemental lectures or office hours useful. I ended up skipping the readings and lectures after the first few weeks.

    • The environments take a long time to run (e.g. >3 hours); this means you have relatively few attempts to modify hyper-parameters which is challenging because there can be 1-2 dozen parameters to tinker with (and there is no working set of parameters supplied).

    • There is little to no 'hands-on' explanatory material. For example, there is little to no guidance on what kinds of diagnostics to use when trying to find a working set of hyper-parameters, and no discussion of how to interpret the results of those diagnostic measures. A short (time-lapsed) example where the presenter walks through finding a working set of hyper-parameters by examining some set of diagnostics on a problem they aren't already familiar with would have been informative.

    • As with ML & DL, the feedback comes late (6-8 weeks into the program), is infrequent (4-5 week periods), last-minute (prior report's feedback arrives 1-2 days before the deadline of the next project), and is often very brief, e.g. ~30-50 words per 8-page report (RL). I have never lost points for being incorrect (although I likely am), but only for omitting required material. The requirements documents are lengthy (>7 pages) and the (graded) requirements are not always condensed into the 'requirements' section.

    • The final environment is not well-constructed; it's a 90s-looking game that runs at 10 FPS on modern hardware, and only on Intel CPUs. If you have a non-intel processor you will need to rent a computer. The school's PACE cluster may be available, but seeing as the project's assigned at the end of the semester (and around Thanksgiving in the fall), you'll likely be competing heavily with other students for access.

    • The final (MCMA) had little to do with the projects which dominated the time I spent on this course. I got a little above average, which was a bit below 50%. If you're dying to do well, review the (later) lectures and skip the textbook.

    I know more about RL after taking the course, but apart from the credential your time may be better spent doing personal research; the David Silver lectures are free, as is the Sutton & Barto textbook. I liked the OpenAI 'Spinning Up' explanations (e.g. for Proximal Policy Optimization [PPO]).

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 24 hours / week

  • mWlYV8RYUV9ANY+ovth3FQ==December 20, 2025summer 2025

    Actually took course in Fall 25, but that option wasn't listed on form.

    Giving the course a 1 because the same feedback is given by almost every student and no attempt has been made to fix it. My feedback is pretty much the same as every other student. Lectures are old and disjoint from projects. No need for the final to be as hard as it is and very taxonomic/unrelated to projects. Ultimately not much better than just seeking out and doing RL projects yourself. For a grade I got near perfect scores on the first three projects and then phoned in the last project and the final. That was enough to get a B, which is all I needed to get reimbursed from my company for the course. Median on the final was ~48%.

    Rating: 1 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • jIrAYmDmWFYSEqjaEU9IlQ==June 6, 2025spring 2025

    This was my first course in the program.

    I passed with a B after failing the final and doing poorly on the first project.

    Overall, projects 2, 3, and 4 are pretty fun to work on. Project one is extremely tedious and not very interesting, but that's more of a personal thing.

    The TAs were extremely helpful and I was fortunate enough to take this with an amazing community of students. However, the course is actively being redesigned and it shows. The lectures (which are hours per week) are completely disjoint from the projects, but certainly not the final.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • q1OtR7SeuWDekbBCKyw0Ig==May 11, 2025spring 2025

    First course of my OMSCS journey. I was in sync with the learnings and lectures for the first month or so, but with so many different papers and algorithms and mainly, the projects, it all went to he'll soon after xD.

    The lectures are quite useless, and overly theoretical, but in the initial stages, did help in building some intuition. The projects were super hard and stimulating, and required 50+ to complete, with a paper-style report for each. I got carried by David Silvers lectures in the first half of the course, after which it was mostly about reading papers and the textbooks.

    Supplementary lectures and reading material were 10/10 and were super relevant to the projects and the theory. The TAs were also super helpful for any doubts(special shoutout to Uzair for the final project) in the office hours. I feel like there should be more emphasis on the code as well for these projects. The current grading system is almost entirely based on the report, which seems weird, considering 90% of the project time is spent in the code and the different things attempted in the code. It kinda makes sense, as we were asked to implement existing algorithms from scratch, but there should be more weight to the coding aspect of the project, than just the report.

    Overall, I enjoyed the course, and enjoyed the gruel it put me through. My final raw % was 83.2 and I was able to bag an A, but mind you, it was a very hard-earned grade. I suggest that people take this course on its own and not along with any other course. I am a full time software engineer so this course really tested my limits. Happy Learning! ;)

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • UVzuF9Uo7P3Zzd/XOmKE/w==May 8, 2025spring 2025

    this is a follow-up to my previous post - "UVzuF9Uo7P3Zzd/XOmKE/w== " pretty lucky that I got an A even though my raw grade is around 80. ultimately I think what happened is that the curve is percentile-based, and half of the class gets an A and the other B. so if you care about grades, it's still fine to take it assuming you can be the top 50% of the surviving students. also like others said, the final was pretty ridiculous and I got ~60. the mcma format is also bad as it incentivizes you to be cautious and not select any choice you are not confident in.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • ErW3R9C1ZwReAtI96bl9Jg==May 8, 2025spring 2025

    I finished with a B.

    Overall, I learned a lot and ready to handle real projects in Reinforcement learning. The reviews here are correct. However, I think with good lectures, the difficulty of this course would drop significantly. I watched the lectures by David Silver and those prepared me for all projects and quizzes but not for the final (that’s why the B). However, the final is wierd anyway and nobody does well, so don’t worry about it too much, make sure you are always above the median in all projects and you will get at least a B.

    The projects are nice but creativity or learnings are not really rewarded, I got points deducted because an important plot was not exactly as they asked it (I think I took training episodes instead of training steps as x axis). So only worry about what they want on the project description, nothing else.

    The LLM to study was terrible, I don’t think this is an effective to study.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • HAguuo5dzJMJGPaXXeclcA==May 5, 2025spring 2025

    I did very, very well for this course (89.5% raw grade without any curve), but I felt that there were a lot of issues that made the learning of crucial RL material difficult:

    1. The lectures were thoroughly lacking in quality, and honestly the banter between the 2 professors was very much unnecessary. What worsens this is that the lectures do not have much connection with any of the 4 projects (which take up a massive 68% of your grade), and after my first project, I realized that watching the lectures were a waste of time and instead focused my attention on the textbooks,

    2. The final exam unfortunately does not serve any pedagogical purpose in this course, and was essentially a test on whether you watched the lectures at all. It serves as the only reason for a student in this class to ever watch the lectures instead of reading the textbook. The mean and median grades for the exam were ridiculously low (< 50%) and further served to show how unnecessary the exam really was. Correct answers were not released as well at the end of the course, which was disappointing. The very generous curve however made sure that getting a median score in the exam would suffice for a good grade, assuming you did decently for the projects.

    3. The final project was plagued with bugs and setup issues. Kudos and respect to the one TA (Uzair) who was single handedly handling the entire project's code base, but this obvious lack of manpower needs to be addressed for this project to be doable. At this point of the semester, most students including myself were burnt out, and we were lucky that the instructional staff were lenient to drop the lowest scoring project out of the 4 required, and I think roughly half the class including myself did not submit anything for this project.

    4. A lack of rubrics for the grading of the papers you submit for the project makes it difficult for students to know what to focus on in the paper, and in RL terms, there is a clear lack of reward signalling / a reward function for the students to learn what constitutes a good or bad report. I was fortunate to do very well for the papers, but I think that perhaps a clearer rubric would also lead to better learning outcomes and not just grades for students in general.

    5. I found that the Ed discussion was very lacking in terms of TA response. The office hours were decent, but if they could achieve the level of office hours like RAIT (AI4R), it would be perfect.

    The course, however, does well on quite a few aspects, which, if barring the above issues, would have made a perfect course for RL:

    1. The textbooks (especially the Grokking supplementary textbook) was excellent for learning. It essentially takes crucial content from the Sutton textbook and makes it easier to digest, and also includes deep RL related concepts that are very well explained.

    2. The projects were very well designed. Projects 1 to 3 (barring Project 4 due to the issues mentioned above) were excellent for learning, and I felt that they were super useful projects that I can use to demonstrate my RL knowledge to any potential employer. Although tedious (most of the results are kind of gate-kept behind hyperparameter tuning especially for Projects 2 and 3), it was very satisfying once one is able to train good RL models and solve the given environments. I would advise students to ensure that their implementation is done correctly first via using sanity checks based on the model's design before moving on to hyperparameter tuning (which will take up a lot of time given the complexity of the model and environments).

    3. The AI Chatbot quizzes were excellent in propping up my understanding of the course concepts learnt, and I reckon this should be present in other OMSCS courses as well due to their excellence. Of course, the quizzes could have been shorter in length and less redundant but were otherwise helpful pedagogically.

    All in all, I would advise people to take this course by itself and be ready to be quite preoccupied with the course due to its challenging nature. It is definitely a rewarding course, but can be better in terms of the lectures and final exam as well as TA engagement.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 18 hours / week

  • zIZzXHYQ2jFLtOKoXRineg==May 4, 2025spring 2025

    This was my first course in the program and it definitely lived up to the hype in terms of difficulty. I originally thought that most of my time would be spent on the lectures or the readings, but quickly realized that the projects require the most time. These take anywhere from 40-60+ hours of work and often meant sacrificing time on lectures/readings. Since this was my first course, I had to spend extra time learning things like writing Latex, creating plots, and using PyTorch. Deep Learning should 100% be a prerequisite to this course. I had minor experience with neural networks before, but the hard part is that you are building them from the ground up along with complicated RL algorithms. This makes it extremely difficult to debug, since the problem can be with your hyperparameters, your neural network, or your RL implementation. I wasted many hours on the projects trying to tune my hyperparameters when there was some stupid bug in my code, making the experiments useless. Overall, I scraped by with an A in the class, despite doing very poorly on the final. If you stick around in the class, and spend a lot of time on the projects and final, you will get at least a B.

    Lectures: I liked the teaching style, but the content drifts farther and farther from RL. Most of the game theory concepts are completely irrelevant. There are so many things that they didn’t cover and it was left for us to figure out.

    P1: I thought this project was great for learning reward shaping and how to implement simple RL algorithms, but found the requirements of the traffic problem to be pretty confusing. P2: This was a great project all around. P3: This one was rly tough, but worthwhile. P4: This had the shortest amount of time, and quite a few technical difficulties. I strongly think that the CNN portion of the code should have been given, or maybe have a video explaining how to implement it. I spent most of the time on this project trying to get that to work, which is NOT how it should be in a RL class.

    Quizzes: The quiz structure was fun and very helpful for understanding aspects of RL that don’t quite click. Final: The final sucked and was stupid. The questions were vague and confusing, and the material was barely covered. Also no study guide? This should be removed from the class.

    For future students, I’d recommend taking DL before this. I’d also recommend making sure you follow the project requirements closely, and explaining the reasoning behind every decision you made. I got my highest project grade having not fully solved the environment. The last thing is that you don’t need the most complicated RL algo to solve most of the problems, so maybe start with something simple before trying PPO.

    For the staff, there is a serious gap between the lectures and the projects. Miguel’s supplementary lectures fill some of that, but not nearly enough. Also, some of the projects should be tweaked to allow more focus on RL rather than DL. And please remove the final exam.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 21 hours / week

  • UVzuF9Uo7P3Zzd/XOmKE/w==April 29, 2025spring 2025

    overall i would say take it for learning purposes and expect to grind hard (each assignment took me 20-50 hours to complete; writing the paper is typically ~5-10 hrs). but be warned that the grading may be harsh and disappointing.

    pros:

    • if you solve every assignment, you will be pretty good at deep rl and have a strong foundation to read & implement latest papers - this is critical for AI right now

    cons:

    • the lectures are a complete waste of time, yet it's what's tested on the final. the content is ancient (not deep rl) and very theoretical (stuff the professors worked on that hasnt taken off since). i stopped watching them 1/3 the way through the course. watch david silver's lectures instead or sergey levine's if you really want to learn RL / DRL
    • the rubric for projects is similar to 7640 ML - it's hidden and sometimes you get a lot of points deducted for stupid reasons (e.g. misnaming a chart). So a small mistake that can be fixed in 1 min ends up costing 5 points vs. the 20 hours you put into tuning the parameters. just insane and extremely demoralizing
    • there is really a lack of efforts from the teaching staff - they send you a lot of materials for reading (books, supplementary lectures, papers) and claim that this is a grad-level class and you should learn things yourself. however, this is more like an excuse - if you compare this to grad-level RL classes from top schools like UCB and Stanford, they provide much greater support to the students (e.g. in the form of pytorch tutorial, going through papers in office hours / ed-discussions, having unit tests in the earlier assignments so people dont waste time). for example, there was an initiative to share 1 new research paper regularly but it stopped after the first time this was announced

    At this point it's not clear if I would get an A or B - you should note that 40% of students withdrew from this class in Fall 2024 since they started revamping this class so grading is not easy.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • 1OH+fPR2qV+jaLfsUVKZdQ==April 24, 2025spring 2025

    I think this is a good topic, but has what I'll charitably call growing pains as a class. As others have mentioned, the lectures aren't very good; they'll get you some basic concepts, but that's it. The projects are interesting, but one of them (P4: deepracer) had substantial technical issues which shortened the effective working time from the already short 3 weeks to an effective week or two once staff fixed the base environment code. I have absolutely no idea what my grade will be. I think my 48 on the final locked me out of any possibility of an A unless the curve is completely insane, but grading's been all like this; report grades seem random.

    Really, just learn it on your own; do some gym examples of increasing complexity, there's no reason to stress and take a poorly constructed final. You'll get at least as much out of that.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • 0xscv0LV93+6dAEzDujOxA==March 1, 2025fall 2024

    I am currently taking the course now. Spring 2025 semester and I thought I did well for project 1 only to receive 30 out of 100. Can I still salvage my grade for rest of the semester and at least get a B.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 37 hours / week

  • RaRXAppLQs8Kf0jWsEO20Q==December 17, 2024fall 2024

    This was my first class and I had a lot of fun during the semester. I took this as first class because I wanted to learn RL for a long time and this was my golden opportunity.

    Pros:

    • The class will set you up for RL research, by the end of it you will be confident to do come up with some ideas yourself.
    • It teaches a lot of RL algorithms from the basics to latest algorithms. (by teaching, I do not mean, there is a lecture covering it, rather, you'll have to learn it to solve the projects, otherwise, you'll fail the project). Be ready to learn cool algorithms like value iteration, Q-learning, SARSA, deep-q-learning, DDPG, TD3, REINFORCE, Vanilla Policy Gradient, PPO, Multi-Agent PPO.
    • Lenient grading. Even if you make some mistakes on your paper, there is not a huge penalty. Even if you don't solve the problem asked in the project, you can explain what all you tried and you should not face huge issues. Analysis is the most important part in the paper. Also explain the problem first in the introduction section.
    • Active discord and communities. Since this is my first class I do not have anything to compare, but I found the class discord to be pretty active. TAs are also active on ed discussions. They mod discord too.
    • Project 1, Project 2 and Project 3 will give you a lot of learning. You will tonnes to talk about if you are interviewing for a RL/ML based roles.
    • You receive detailed feedback on your papers, which if you implement will set you up for easy A.
    • Grades are revealed in 3 weeks after submission.
    • Regrade is fairly quick, 1 week.
    • The instructor and TAs are quite knowledgeable and very nice people in general. Joe and Markian top tier TAs.
    • OH are helpful, they also have private 1/1 OH, which really helped me in the initial projects. They also provide recording for public OH.

    Cons:

    • Totally disaster ed lectures, but they are needed for final exams. My strategy was to clear my concepts from David Silver Lectures (gold-standard) and learn game theory stuff from ed lectures. TBH, game theory ed-lectures are decent. There are two professors in the lectures who are top of the top in the field, but the lectures are setup in a way that they communicate with one another to teach stuff, which doesn't work. Not to mention, a lot of jokes in the lectures, which is just distracting and borderline irritating. Imagine preparing for finals and listening to jokes while trying to focus.
    • Towards the deadline for submissions, many students ask question on ed, and then TAs get overwhelmed and hence some questions take longer than others to get answered. My strategy if my question was not answered was to create another private post for TAs, as a reminder, then the answers came.
    • Project 4, I did not learn much from this project, it was quite basic. There was also a issue with AWS credits. This is the first semester they were running this project, so problems were expected. But the fact that this did not add anything new to the learning from what was an amazing class until project 3 was sad. I was expecting something more challenging in Project 4. It disappointed a lot of the students.
    • Final exam: Its the most weird exam I have ever taken and it is given a weightage of 30%. It multiple choice and multiple answer (credit for partially correct answer is given). Its important to do at least average to get an A.

    Recommendation: if you are okay by learning by yourself. Its a fun class.

    Suggestion: Use OH and discord community to the fullest. Overall I got high 90s in all my projects, and above average in finals, and got an easy A.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • kCewUHAwOk4/Bff0djmMJg==December 16, 2024fall 2024

    The course consists of 4 projects and 1 final. I like the projects, and think they are well related to reality. However, I didn't like the lectures, because I think it is for those who have some or very good foundations of ML. Since it is my first ML course, it is difficult for me to understand some concepts mentioned in the lecture. Not recommended for those who have no ML knowledge.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • 4r+C/SIbKMb8nRfLk1VlQg==August 7, 2024summer 2024

    TLDR; This class is very cool. Project 3 is very hard. If you're interested in the material, you should for sure take it.

    This is a really cool course. The lectures are very similar quality to ML, so if you liked ML and the corny way Prof. Littman and Isbell lecture you will like this. I found the lectures to be fine. They cover the material, but there is a lack of depth that you are expected to extrapolate from the assigned readings. The other thing to note is that the lectures do not really cover material that is necessary for Project 2 and 3. Project 1 was really easy after the horror that was the 10-page papers from ML. If you want to do well read the paper literally everything you need is in that paper. Project 2 was also pretty easy. I don't recall if they gave the best advice about papers to read and reference for that, but look up DQN, DDPG, PPO, or REINFORCE and you should be able to figure it out. Project 3 was very difficult. This project covered MARL in a simplified Overcooked environment. And a big part of why this assignment is hard is because the environment is implemented in python and slow. Start this one as soon as it drops. Clear your calendar for the three weeks the assignment is available. I did not solve all of the layouts, but the penalty for not doing so is "small" (they don't specify what it is exactly). The HW is all very easy as well. I would recommend getting them out of the way quickly so that you can spend all your energy on the papers. The Final exam for this class is pretty annoying. There was no provided study guide and no clear communication of what would be on it. But don't worry because as long as you're ahead of the average you should be in good shape. Honestly, feels kinda disingenuous to have a final exam that is multi-choice/ multi-answer for a class that almost entirely focuses on analysis rather than definitions and rote memorization, but I also understand that grading a free response exam would take a lot of time. I got right around 50% and still got an A, so unless you really screwed up your projects or missed a homework I wouldn't sweat it too much.

    I'm giving this course a 5 on quality because it was awesome, but recognize this is a graduate level course where most of the learning is done outside of lecture. TAs as always are going to give really vague and unhelpful answers to questions that get to the heart of assignments. I'm giving a 4 on difficulty because I'd say that is the average difficulty level, but Project 3 is seriously no joke and has been the hardest assignment I've worked on at OMS so far.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • OurjdTA81rEMUz00lB7TeA==June 6, 2024spring 2024

    The lectures for this course were some of the better ones I've seen in OMSCS, of course there is always room for improvement and to teach more state of the art material, but overall was still extremely solid. The assignments were also pretty useful for the most part, especially the projects. I like classes like these where it requires a good deal of time and work BUT the assignments are designed in such a way where you actually learn a TON. Granted, a lot of the learning was done on the projects from my own research, but I also feel like they graded much fairer than other courses (CV and ML4T) and the assignments were not nearly as tedious as other classes (CV). The information learned in doing the assignments and projects was great in my opinion, especially if you have an interest in RL. I wish that continuous action spaces were covered more, along with a more formalized learning of multi-agent RL and how to use RL libraries (since everything was implemented from scratch except NN neurons and backprop). The projects, especially projects 2 and 3, require an insane amount of time so plan accordingly to start VERY early. I got a 100% on the third project but turned it in late and got a late penalty since I didn't allocate my time correctly. I highly recommend this class as it is probably the best one of the 8 courses I have taken.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 3 / 5Workload: 16 hours / week

  • 3DAMyikTEhmL6xCnN9OIKw==May 9, 2024spring 2024

    I had taken AI and DL prior to RL, and I would rank the difficulty of the classes such that RL > AI > DL. I'm very glad that I took RL LAST because this was the class where I underestimated how much effort it would take to absorb the material, let alone just be able to do the assignments! RL is very conceptual and math-heavy, so if you do not understand how and why the math works then you're in for a world of hurt.

    Student performance is judged based on 30% Homework (Gradescope submission work), 45% Projects (code to GitHub and research-style paper submissions), and 25% Final Exam (one closed-book exam proctored by HonorLock for 120 min).

    Homework - Other people in OMSCentral have already remarked on the Homework assignments, so I have nothing new to contribute here. Seems unchanged from previous years for Spring 2024.

    Project #1 - I highly recommend to put most of your effort on writing a high-quality research-like report for each project and not being fixated on mimicking the exact results of a research paper and/or getting the "best possible" results for each experiment. I learned this from Project 1 where even though I was able to get results very similar to Sutton's paper of TD Lambda, I still got a 10 point deduction for "not having sufficiently in-depth analysis" in my 5 page paper. To be fair, I agreed with this comment from the grader - I did not know how to best analyze the "whys" of what I was seeing and was not explaining it in a sufficient depth. I am pretty sure the deduction would have been far less if I had been unable to mimic the results of Sutton's paper but was able to explain the theoretical "whys" of why failures happened in my experiments.

    Project #2 - This time around, I quickly coded up a solution that I thought worked sufficiently to address the Lunar Lander AI problem and spent over 1 week writing a report that elaborated much more on the "why" analysis of my experiments. There were pros and cons to this approach, but I wouldn't realize it until Project 3. The pros was that I got zero deductions on Project 2 (full score), which meant my report was solid), and I knew then exactly how yo format any future reports that I would ever need to turn in for RL. The con was that my agent for Project 2 was not the most efficient, and this would later affect me in Project 3.

    Project #3 - This was only the 2nd time that the course had students work on a multi-agent problem that was the Overcooked AI problem, and I could tell they were still observing where they should fix any kinks if they were to have Project 3 remain the same for future semesters. This project made me sick...literally! I spent over 3 weeks spending all my free time reading more than 30 research papers and attempting so many different experiments in order to get some results for a writeup. I lost sleep, had constant headaches, etc. I was not looking for perfection of being able to solve all 5 layouts of Overcooked, but I did panic when I realized that I was getting stuck just solving 2 out of the 5 layouts and not making any progress despite my efforts. The teaching staff recommended that as a baseline people try using their single-agent approach from Project 2 to solve Project 3 and see how far they get before attempting any other new multi-agent approaches. This is where my con from Project 2 affected me - since I didn't spend countless hours making the most efficient agent, the agent from Project 2 was only able to solve 2 layouts in Project 3. I spent the next 3 weeks trying new algorithms (and failing), and then resorting back to trying to salvage my agent from Project 2 to solve at least one more layout so that it wouldn't have such a pathetic performance. I ended up being able to only solve 3 out of 5 layouts with my final implementation, but I wrote what I thought was a high quality paper explaining the whys of my successes and failures. Ultimately, I got very small deductions for not being able to solve the remaining 2 layouts and got a higher score than I did in Project 1. The class everage and median for Project 3 was low (in the 70s), and I think this is due to 2 potential reasons. Either people spent all their time doing experiments but didn't put the same amount of focus into their reports, OR people procrastinated and thought they could spend just a few days before the deadline to get everything done (code and report) and quickly realized that Project 3 is 10x harder than both Project 1 and 2 combined. I personally think that if I had spent more time in Project 2 making the most optimal agent possible, then I would have better experimental success in Project 3 because enhancing a very efficient single-agent approach to work for the multi-agent environment would have shaved off a lot of experimentation time for me.

    Final Exam: I needed the high scores from Projects and Homework to save me. I failed this exam so terribly that if you were to look at my performance in relation to the rest of the class for just the exam, it looked like I did not learn a darn thing all semester! I studied and prepared for 7 days prior to the exam, and still "failed". My advice is to not over-prepare by studying the lecture videos - instead, read the relevant chapters from the class textbook and then just take the test. I spent too much time going over lecture videos and not enough time reading the high-level perspectives from the textbook. Also, blind guessing results in points deduction because the final exam is multiple choice with multiple answers for some questions. If you choose 1 wrong choice and 1 right choice for a multiple-answer question, then you get 0 points on that question!

    Lecture videos - So awful...this is the first class where I felt like vomiting everytime I started a video to hear the non-stop bantering and joking of the 2 professors who star in the lecture videos. The joking got in the way of me learning anything at all, and they would purposely spend hours deriving something trivial and then wiping the math away to say they deliberately made errors to prove a point. It got to the point where I stopped watching the weekly allotment of videos for 1 month because I couldn't sit through all the unhelpful chatter of the videos. My feedback to the teaching staff was to please just do away with the current lecture videos because they were the worst part of the course (even worse than the experience of taking the final exam).

    Teaching staff: This class is so darn RESPONSIVE! This is the first class where the TAs explained their background and hobbies on a Ed post at the beginning of the semester, and it was so nice to know these people were "real people" and not just names on a virtual board that answered questions or graded assignments. Some of the TAs had IMPRESSIVE backgrounds, and I found myself wondering "wow, how high is the caliber of expectations to be able yo qualify as a TA for RL?!". Also, the TAs are hovering around Ed Discussion around the clock, and responses to student questions are posted within a few hours. That is such a crazy difference compared to AI where questions from students could go unanswered for up to 7 days! The TA in charge of Project 3 also spent a lot of time asking for feedback about how to improve Project 3 for the future. The way the TAs helped to run the course is how all classes in OMSCS should run.

    Extra Credit: We had an extra credit opportunity this semester, and I did not get it. But if I did, it would have added an extra 10 points to my grade on Project 3. I guess in the end I didn't really need any extra credit because I lost very few points on Project 3 despite dismal experimentation results (solving 3 out of 5 layouts is still just solving 60% of a problem, which is really not something to rave about if I were on a podium).

    Conclusion: I learned SO much, and also worked SO much for Project 3 that I need to find a passion project to showcase everything that I can do now with reinforcement learning! Even though I failed the Final Exam, my performance on Homework and Projects was high enough to still result in an A (with the help of the class curve). I had estimated that this would be a B grade class for me since it took so much effort for me to understand the material, and therefore I was pleasantly surprised to see my final grade in the class!

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • YcRCrpL/0MAbjrBk0BQjiA==December 17, 2023fall 2023

    Very difficult, but in the right ways. This class treats you like an adult and puts you in charge of managing your time and research to get to the finish line. Probably the first class in OMSA that fostered a collegial relationship with TAs (10 classes taken).

    Challenging but achievable, and very engaging because of this. Just finishing the class won't get you an A, unlike many other OMSA courses. The projects are hard and you'll have to do some research of your own to succeed. But it's a process that will help you learn a lot in very practical ways. If RL is at all interesting to you, don't miss this class - just take it alone or pair it with something easy.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • Op880d/6dofv1aYhkGTiRw==December 13, 2023fall 2023

    One thing that holds this course back is that there is an amount of disconnection between the lectures and everything else. The lectures follow the mathematical theory behind how the various RL topics work. Then the projects and weekly homework focus on practical applications. In between those two ends, you have the readings -- they should bridge the gap, but often fall short.
    The readings consist of the Sutton and Barto book, which I did enjoy, and then research papers. I recommend buying the hard copy of the book because it is foundational and I found it handy to write in the margins and highlight. I'll be returning to it during self-study a lot. The research publications are just okay. Some are far more recent than others, and while the older papers provide some much needed insight into how the RL paradigm has shifted over the past 10+ years, the last third of the class is definitely in need of renovation. For example, the third project focuses on multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). It's been said before, but it is an absolute giant of a project that tested me in so many different ways. You would think that multi-agent learning would make up some portion of lectures, right? Negative. The last third of the class lectures focus on a smattering of topics, mostly game theory. While game theory was interesting, the MARL domain is so relative today that it was boggling that it wasn't covered in much detail. That was pushed to a lot of self-study centered around Project 3: we were given a laundry list of papers to read, pick an algorithm(s) to implement, and build a MARL solution to a cooking environment. It didn't help that there was a bug in the environment that I got conflicting responses about what it affected. Overall, this is a difficult, math-heavy course. It is in need of modernization. The lectures are dense, and sometimes confusing, and the interplay between the two lecturers is grating at times. When you just want things explaining simply, it can get frustrating. There needs to be a better bridge between theory and practice. Get ready to spend ungodly amounts of time looking at other resources, like the fantastic David Silver lectures or alternative textbooks. The TAs are great; they're just working within the framework that they've been given. I enjoyed the first two projects, and yes the final exam is very hard. I scored in the 50s and was above the course average so it is universally difficult.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • mJKb1Qa6lGYdomsFC3NlvA==December 11, 2023fall 2023

    If you were inspired to take reinforcement learning after hearing that it was used to train ChatGPT, avoid this course, because you’ll learn none of it unless you self-study, in which case you might as well not take the module at all. IMO, the short reinforcement learning section in ML4T is more applicable to real-life than the entire 7642 syllabus.

    Lectures are absolutely useless except for Final Exam, and maybe HW1: • Charles is fine but he only teaches a negligible proportion of the syllabus • Michael is incomprehensible?? More than once he switches train of thought mid-sentence, and he jumps from concept to concept without any context or explanation. The bantering style of lecturing exacerbates this • Lecturing style aside, the syllabus is so theoretical, it teaches you absolutely nothing about how to implement anything, much less best practices in modern RL. This is a massive wasted opportunity because RL is super interesting (as I found out during self-study)

    My recommendation is to read the relevant textbook chapters before watching the lectures, then the lectures might make a bit more sense. The rest of the weekly readings are mostly useless except for the final exam: • Believe it or not, the paper introducing deep RL is not even in the reading list. In fact, I don’t think any deep RL papers are on the reading list • Most readings were authored in the 1990s, i.e. were focused on solving simplistic computer games. It’s hard to see any application to real life • Project 3 has 12 suggested readings in it, all of which are 2016 or later, covering more recent techniques in RL. Why aren’t these in the weekly readings instead?

    TAs are insanely helpful & trying their absolute best. I cannot stress this enough. I feel like they themselves know that the course content is useless, but there’s only so much they can do.

    Project 3 will have you implementing a multi-agent model (which they don’t teach). As others have already mentioned, this project is an absolute beast, and combining multiple neural nets in 1 model can be very very tricky in PyTorch. Clear all other deliverables ASAP, and get started the moment it’s released.

    Finals-wise, know that for multiple semesters, the average grade has been <50%. You’ll need a super deep understanding & memorization of the attributes and properties of the whole syllabus (which is massive). Most of the questions don’t have answers explicitly stated in the lectures or readings. And cheatsheets aren’t allowed.

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 30 hours / week

  • ZN9uVRB/TlU1+5KZLa6fUA==October 26, 2023fall 2023

    Super hard course, haha check out my candid review here! https://youtu.be/LEwjFYt4XIQ

    Rating: 2 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 15 hours / week

  • bGMsiYUbTvuCh+jf02bt7w==August 10, 2023summer 2023

    I took this course over the summer (doing my first week of homework on a cruise ship in the Mediterranean) with full knowledge that it is a tough class, but observing that the grade distribution for the class in the summer (https://lite.gatech.edu/lite_script/dashboards/grade_distribution.html) is generally about the same as for the fall and spring. Sure enough, this semester the distribution came out to be 41% A, 28% B, 1% C, 2% D, 2% F, 25% W.

    I rate this class only a 3 because the lectures are poor, dated, and lacking relevance. The lectures contain many mistakes which aren't all annotated. Many results are presented only at a high level so that it is difficult to understand the meaning or how to practically apply the results. The continual banter, including debates about the meanings of things, adds to confusion. The lectures don't contain any material on policy gradient methods even though these are arguably the most important and commonly applied techniques in modern RL. It is also annoying that project 3 is all about policy gradient multi-agent methods for which the lectures provide no support at all. As other people have, I recommend the David Silver lectures on Youtube.

    One unexpected downside of taking this class over the summer was that only 160 students were enrolled so that there was no critical mass for good Slack discussions. Consequently, everyone gave up on Slack and questions posted there went unanswered. You get better support from other students by taking the class in the Fall or Spring. Fortunately, I had manually copied the Slack discussions from the spring into a Word doc to preserve these before I'd lose access. Having these available and searchable was useful.

    As other people have said, project 3 is a beast and really represents a capstone project even though the grade allocation is the same as for projects 1 and 2. I finished project 2 and the homeworks early so I'd have 3 weekends on project 3. I also took some days off work, ending up spending about 90 hours on the project.

    I was able to significantly beat the baselines on project 3 using CCPPO (following the ray lib example), increasing the size of the central critic NN (because coordinated value estimation is hard), and with lots of reward shaping to incentivize better and more aggressive defense. Reward-shaping is tricky and easily becomes pathological, so this tip may not be much of a tip since it takes a ton of training experimentation to get this working.

    Watch the video visualizations generated by the provided Jupyter notebook. The best baseline (3) wins nearly all games in which the agent team starts with the ball (the odd episodes), but ties most games in which they do not (the even episodes). So the best strategy for improving on this may be to find a way to add an aggressive defense and try to steal the ball and also score on the even episodes. I did this mostly by adding reward shaping to incentivize more aggressive defense. I found this wasn't enough with PPO alone: I needed an algorithm with more coordination.

    For my setup, I used docker on my Windows 11 host system using an RTX3090 GPU and a CPU with 20 cores and 64gb ram. Lots of people had trouble with cloud setups and some people were hardly able to complete any training at all, spending most of their time troubleshooting problems and complaining about this on Ed. Following a suggestion someone made in Slack the previous semester. I was able to speed up training significantly by increasing the SGD minibatch size and related parameters, and also the learning rate--so that I could complete a 20 million step training run in around 10 hours (instead of 24).

    The final exam was an exercise in remembering details from the lectures and two papers (KWIK and Sutton 88). I wish I had made a table of methods/techniques/algorithms and their properties and memorizing this as someone suggested in another review. I studied by rewatching all of the lectures and making notes, and studying the posted student answers to study questions, finding other sources for answers when those were weak. This study was enough to get me a score above the upper quartile, but I think I could have done better with added table memorization. The mean score was 46, median 48, and upper quartile 56.

    I ended up with an A, scoring (94,95,97) on the projects, and a 61 on the final.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • Pqc0oBP00i24fWaUAIuZDQ==May 12, 2023spring 2023

    Reinforcement Learning Review

    Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a fascinating class, but I have mixed feelings now that the course has concluded. On the plus side, RL feels like it just might be the next "big thing." The field is a fascinating fusion of classical computer science, statistics, cognitive and neuroscience, and mathematics. Before taking this class and having taken the AI, ML4T, and ML courses, this class felt like what AI should be, or at least, what I expected AI and machine learning to be like; that is, an agent that explores an environment, acquiring feedback and evaluating its progress towards some goal at each step along the way. The agent-centric aspect of RL distinguishes it from other areas of AI and ML, and it is a novel way to think about problems in this space.

    On the downside, RL is still very theoretical and has yet to gain traction in industry, as it is not applicable to most commercial use cases. The domains where RL has been successful have been confined mainly to robotics and gaming - although I have heard that large language models like chatGPT used specific RL techniques. RL is an active area of research, and I hope it will find broader appeal as success in new domains and use cases accrue. RL is definitely something to keep your eye on if you are in the Machine Learning or Interactive Intelligence specializations.

    There are three projects (45%), six homeworks (30%), and a final exam (25%) that make up the final grade. There are course lectures, office hours, and a lot of readings. This class is demanding, and the projects require a lot of time. In terms of effort and workload, it is up there with ML and AI, and I easily spent at least 40-60 hours on each project. Preparing for the final was chaotic, and I was "burned out" after P3. The instructors included a series of game theory lectures at the end. Including this material was a little puzzling, at least to me, since none of the homework or assignments involved game theory. However, conceptually there is an overlap between game theory, a la John Nash's "a beautiful mind" equations, and multi-agent RL.

    I finished with a final grade of 74%, which was a B. I did below average on the first project and the exam and was at or above the median for the rest. With a little effort, you can get 100s on all HWs - the first one is the hardest, and they get progressively easier and less time-consuming. Understand how to write a good report for the projects - results are less important than showcasing your understanding. For the final, don't overthink it - know the big picture. The average/mean was around 46-48% (I scored a 42%), but it is easy to go down the wrong path in studying since there is so much material. I certainly did.

    Lectures and Class Material The lectures for this class could be better. I did not enjoy them and stopped watching them halfway thru. Some are good, while other modules were just confusing. The focus was mainly on mathematical proofs and lacked a coherent narrative. I don't think the course modules were helpful for the final - they went into too much detail.

    I watched most of the David Silver lectures, which were terrific. The David Silver lectures were coherent and well-paced, each one building on the previous one in a coordinated fashion. The course lectures were less connected and felt arbitrarily stitched together in comparison. Often it was unclear why a proof or equation was being introduced or how it fit into a larger RL context. I often felt "lost in the details." Combing both the Silver lectures and the course lectures is likely the best approach since the Silver lectures provide a layout while using the course lectures to fill in the mathematical details whenever needed - but who has the time to watch so many videos?

    Many of the readings were excellent. After you bang your head for a while on an academic paper, it does eventually begin to make sense. I learned quite a bit from the papers and the book. I didn't read everything, and some readings were harder to grasp or seemed to fringe, but the course designers did an excellent job selecting the essential readings in the field. The DQN journal articles and the Sutton paper on TD learning are foundational readings. The Barto and Sutton text was also quite good, at least the early chapters (i.e., 1 thru 7) I had read.

    The TAs for the class were talented and generally impressive, particularly the lead instructors. They were very positive and supportive, but your YMMV for the office hours. The TAs preferred facilitating class discussions, letting things flow organically, instead of a more structured guided office hour. Often, I felt they were reluctant to provide solid answers or advice, instead encouraging students to give it their best shot and not be afraid to take some liberties or explore the topic independently.

    Ed discussions were good - lots of class participation but not too much direct involvement from the TAs. Again, they preferred to stay in the background and referee more than actively participate. But they did help direct and clarify topics when necessary.

    Overall a great class and an important and emerging area for those interested in AI. It gets a bit theoretical at times, but the projects were fantastic and provided the necessary hands-on RL experience I was looking for. If you want to prepare for the class, study a bit of dynamic programming and read up on and understand the grid-world MDP problem using value and policy iteration. Learning the basics of PyTorch will come in handy. Understand gradient functions (or read Sutton's Temporal Difference Learning paper). Also, I would learn how to spin up and configure a virtual machine using one of the major cloud providers - that may come in handy for P3 if you don't own a souped-up home machine. Finally, if you have a lot of spare time, watch David Silver's lectures, read his DQN paper, and the first 5 or 6 chapters of Sutton.

    Good luck.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • V3XDGwLyDdCA+/FluLIh3Q==May 9, 2023spring 2023

    I won't spend a lot of time re-iterating stuff that other people already accurately described. TAs good (and very nice, can't praise them enough), assignments very easy, lots of reading, projects generally hard. I'll just provide some more insight into project 3. You could probably submit this project as the capstone project for DL, the one that you have multiple months to do and must complete with a group, and I'm very sure the TAs would OK it; it's that complex. If you aren't intimately familiar with RLLib, I would strongly, strongly recommend spending time earlier in the semester looking through the source code and the examples it contains. Learn how to modify the structure of a neural network or the overall behavior of one of the pre-existing algorithms. That was probably what most of us spent most of our time doing, and it's a real shame, because that cuts into valuable experimentation time. Also, have a fairly modern 6 (really 8) core CPU and 32 GB of RAM MINIMUM if you don't want to be renting a VM from someone. I know everyone always says "start early," which is often not even an option for a lot of people, but really do clear some time for this one, if possible.

    That said, I think grading was more lenient on P3 than the other projects. The median ended up higher, while the mean was lower. I did quite well with, and I can't stress this enough, garbage results. They give a lot of points for demonstrating one's understanding of the principles behind multi-agent RL, and for thoroughly explaining why one's agents performed in the manner they did.

    The final was extremely dumb. Assuming they keep the multiple-choice format, my recommendation would would be to simply re-watch the lectures, making note of any time they define something or describe the performance characteristics of some algorithm. (how well it does/what kind of environments it can operate in/what information it needs in order to work). You should be able to beat the curve with that alone. They provided a lot of study questions in Ed, but I wish I spent less time on them, as rote memorization would have almost certainly earned me a better score.

    I hope they are able to improve/streamline P3, because it seems like a project with a lot of potential that was simply too difficult and complicated for most people to get working and then have time to conduct detailed experimentation in a timeframe of 3-4 weeks (Again, start early if you can. Neglect your children if you have to!). I think the idea was to give people this powerful RL framework with which would allow them to spend less time implementing and more time experimenting, but I think the opposite ended up happening.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 30 hours / week

  • BsqWiA2rx6PVxOKvP6tViA==May 8, 2023spring 2023

    This class is pretty great and feels like a grad class. The homeworks are easy expect HW1 which I found to be pretty stressful and difficult. I will say the TAs were pretty amazing throughout the entire class and were very supportive. A lot of students too were very involved which made for a fun time as a fellow student.

    The projects are all good:

    Project 1- Pretty easy to follow pseudo code from the book and other students discuss little tricks they had to do to get the exact results on here as well as in your slack.

    Project 2- Fun project, I used a DQN. Feels sort of out of left field to be learning some DL, but once you get past that hump its not too hard. Still a lot of work and time to train and tune, but its pretty fun to do and overall enjoyable.

    Project 3- New project as of the last year or two. Really really really hard. I was not able to beat the baselines we had to train against despite trying all sorts of strategies. I trained locally, and you need at least 18gbs of memory to train your model. Spent lots of time and effort on this and felt bad to not get good results. Most of my time was spent scouring github libraries for MARL to try and understand how to implement a decent agent and make it work in RLLIB.

    All of these projects require you to start the minute they open otherwise your chances of finishing them and getting a good grade is very slim.

    Exam- The exam was total BS. Some of the questions were about an algorithm that we never talked about. The review questions did not really prepare you. The TAs were suppose to post answers but they never did. While I liked the idea of us learning off each other, please also post the answers!

    I also tried to take it at my parents house while on vacation so I bombed it as I rushed through it. Oh well, my exam grade doesn't reflect how much I learned. I had pretty much aced every single homework and project and still did so incredibly poor on the final.

    Lectures- I didn't mind the jokey tone that smoov and curly took in this one, but a few of the topics in the middle where just like straight math proofs with no real explanation of how they connect to the topics. It was fine, and I would never say the lectures are bad, but I wouldn't say they blew me away either. As others have mentioned David Silver's lectures are much better and I would recommend watching those. Like a good grad class, you will need to do a lot of learning outside of the provided materials. In this class it's cool to read papers that are cutting edge and interesting, and be able to understand them.

    Overall- TAs were really good. TJ and Jake in particular (as well as others!) who ran our office hours were always very supportive. It's a fun, challenging class that despite the final I'd take again.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • w8O28GZbi4QsOKRokvPQ3w==May 8, 2023spring 2023

    This is probably the most difficult course I've taken in my entire academic career. Thankfully, the difficulty isn't artificial and doesn't come from needless busywork, but rather from the intensity of the material itself.

    The course is broken down into six homeworks, three projects, and one exam. The six homeworks are auto-graded in Gradescope and cover the material from the lectures/readings and help give a good foundation for thinking about the projects. The projects are interesting in that you are not graded on your implementation at all, but rather on your interpretation of your results, and the exam is a multiple choice exam that covers all of the material in the course.

    The homeworks don't have anything of note in them that makes them stand out - they're fun and interesting, but a bit foundational. Once you crack the main idea of the problem, the implementation is generally straightforward.

    The projects are real work. You'll end up writing a ton of code and submitting it, but it's basically only checked to make sure it runs and isn't a copy of another implementation. The effort comes from writing the reports and coming up with justifications for how you approached the problem, and (more importantly) showing that you understood the problem. My undergraduate degree was a writing degree, and these projects felt like a real synthesis of all of my skills. I enjoyed the process, though I generally spent a single day writing the report, which I wouldn't recommend unless you have experience writing a 15 page paper in 10 hours. It melted me and affected basically everything else in my life.

    One thing to mention about the projects is that the work starts hard and gets mind-blowingly difficult. The third project leans heavily on compute power and understanding the RLlib abstraction, and if you wander off in the woods for any amount of time, you will likely not be able to solve the problem. I was not able to beat the benchmarks, mainly because I had a significant amount of difficulty understanding the RLlib abstraction and figuring out how to write and debug my own code. I spent about 100 hours on the project, but beat the median only by a little bit, mainly because I wasn't able to come up with excellent points because I didn't fully understand what was going on. It was humbling, but (most importantly), it made me thirsty for more.

    The final, though, was awful. They give you a study guide with open-ended problems, but don't give you the answers they think are right. Given that the students argue over definitional true/false questions, this makes it really difficult to study and know if you are on the right track. Couple that with the fact that the exam doesn't reflect what was in those study guides, and the average for the final was about a 45 and the highest grade was an 80.

    I don't think the exam was difficult enough to warrant this, and the main problem with it was the lack of getting the students prepared. I think they can solve this by offering gigantic question banks they draw from for the exam, and offering guidance on how to approach the problems.

    Outside of the exam, this course was excellent and felt like a real graduate course (instead of undergraduate+, like GIOS and ML4T) as the focus was on interpretation and analysis rather than code.

    I would recommend this course, but understand there are points where you're likely going to want to give up and/or cry and/or hallucinate from lack of sleep.

    Rating: 4 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 30 hours / week

  • h/Zgy6NlXCdw2FxeSESgyA==January 2, 2023fall 2022

    Heavy workload if taking another course, TAs are engaged and not snarky, 2 office hours sessions a week, covers the classics, but also gets into the newer DRL stuff. Lots of papers to read and process, which is fun and feels more like a graduate class than some other courses.

    Assignment grading is slow, which seems by design (given there is no shortage of TAs). You just have to have confidence that you're doing the right things.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 4 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • YSmR2Wa53yPIhwqoL+t8Xw==December 22, 2022summer 2022

    It is the best class that I took in OMSCS so far. You definitely learn a lot. The topics are eye opening. It is harder than AI, ML and DL imo.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 29 hours / week

  • 9NOUVXJGwP8+h/Gi8ZkrHg==December 21, 2022fall 2022

    Overall - It is the most insightful and difficult course I've taken so far (KBIA, ML4T, AI4R), which is to be expected from the reviews. Loved project 1 and project 2, reading papers and trying to implement TD(lambda) and DQN was super fun!

    Project 3, I have no idea what I was doing. I feel like it was such a huge step up from P1 and P2, and I was not making any progress in any meaningful way. rllib is a monster of abstraction which is pretty much the crux of the project. I would recommend anyone taking this course in the future, try to implement P2 in rllib after doing it in just PyTorch. Also, you'll need a machine that is good enough the train for P3. I know the TAs did not explicitly say this but if you can't train 1 million timesteps within 1 hour, you need a better/faster machine. This is the ONLY way to know that what you are doing is making an impact on the agent.

    Homework was trivial, given that this is not your python course, you'll be fine.

    The Final was brutal. As stated in various posts, the average was in the 40s. I rewatched the entire lecture videos, read various sections of the book, and did the practice exam twice. After all of that, I still got the average.

    Time spent per week: 20+

    Conclusion: ML specialization, this is a must. Even if ML is not your specialization, you might as well take it. Everything else will be a walk in the park.

    Rating: 5 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 25 hours / week

  • Ibg01OgkOuJlAKeU+X2d9Q==December 20, 2022fall 2022

    Very interesting and engaging material, but extremely time consuming course. Would not recommend taking another course at the same time if you're a part time student. I found the lectures in the course material hard to follow and keep up with, the David Silver lectures on YouTube cover a good chunk of the same material and I found those much easier to understand. The final project is very computationally demanding, you should have a decent GPU, a high end CPU, and at least 64GB of RAM available you if want to train on your own system. After dedicating over 90% of my free time after my job to this course, I was able to pull off a final grade of B.

    Rating: 3 / 5Difficulty: 5 / 5Workload: 20 hours / week

  • pEfU6w1LKfuEWEoJdlEXqw==December 18, 2022fall 2022

    It was my 10th course in OMSCS. Some feedback may not contain too many details due to the large class size. Nonetheless, this course is well-managed by TAs.

    1. Homework. HW1-6s are simple and easy to implement. HW5-6 may require additional readings regarding KWIK and game theory.

    2. Projects. They are most time-consuming. I didn't work well in Project 1 but achieved 100 in Project 2 and Project 3. The key is to explain your methods, experiments, and analysis thoroughly. That's the reason why TAs recommend IEEE templates. The main structure of the analysis is describing phenomena -- providing hypotheses -- validating your conclusion theoretically and experimentally. IEEE papers usually separate experiments and results. But I recommend combining them because of the 5-pages limitation. It is also convenient to read and prune. Besides, I strongly recommend following Kaiming He's writing style in the Masked Autoencoders paper. You can highlight or bold key concepts at the beginning of paragraphs.

    Project 1 focuses on TD-lambda. You need to introduce TD methods, replicate experiments and provide your own analysis. Showing your understanding of each experiment is the crux. But I couldn't realize it until submission.

    Project 2 is about deep reinforcement learning, specifically, the DQN series. Two experiments include the training phase and hyperparameter-tuning. It is easy to talk a lot about hyperparameters, like model architecture, learning rate, discount factor, and epsilon-greedy. However, don't forget to describe your training result and analysis in detail.

    Project 3 introduces multi-agent reinforcement learning and plays a Google research football game. The provided baselines depend on PPO. PPO is such a robust algorithm that you may fail to achieve some improvements. So I suggest using QMIX, MAPPO, or other SOTA methods directly because each trial may take several days (So sorry that I was not able to reply to some questions in Edstem). I trained MAPPO on Google cloud and paid 50 dollars plus 300 free credits. Although it is manageable to use GCP with only the free credits, the high computing requirements (especially CPUs) cause much trouble in coding and debugging. For RLlib-based implementation, I recommend seeing some GitHub extensions, such as epymarl and MARLlib. As for the report, except for win-rate improvements, you need to select or customize parameters to display team coordination.

    1. Exam. Important Relevance: Sutton & Barto's book > David Silver's slides > lecture videos. Although lecture videos don't cover all concepts, you can still achieve an above-average grade by only watching them.
